title: date if it aids in identification . • Metadata and Ontologies: In archives and a
content:
date if it aids in identification . • Metadata and Ontologies: In archives and academia, the context lives in metadata. Standards like Dublin Core or METS define common fields (Title, Creator, Date, Format, Identifier, etc.) so that even if you flatten a whole library’s file structure, each file carries tags that situate it in the larger ontology of knowledge. For example, all images might be tagged with a controlled vocabulary of subjects or an ontology of media types. This is relevant to MythOS: rather than relying on deeply nested folders for context (which can become brittle), one can use a flatter file structure combined with rich metadata tagging to achieve organization. Queries or tools can then virtually regroup or filter content by those tags (similar to how a media library app works).
In a unified MythOS archive, we might see files named with short opaque IDs or standardized codes, while a “codex” (master index) maps those to meaningful descriptions. Incorporating a persistent ID like a DOI for important items (e.g. final outputs or published content in one’s life-log) could even allow external citation or easy tracking of those items over decades.
Large-Scale Simulation & Worldbuilding Frameworks: Global Names and Layered Scenes
context: From document: Transforming a Fragmented Digital Life into a Unified Canonical Architecture — chunk 8 (chunk 8/37)
